sentence: Subjective:
Randy Gutierrez, a 1-year-old male, presents with a 1-day history of symptoms.
He started to feel unwell around supper time last night, with the onset of a runny nose, stuffiness, and cough.
The patient's mucus is currently clear.
He has been pulling on his ears and rubbing his nose.
His rectal temperature was 100.3°F.
He ate his dinner last night but had a poor appetite this morning, consuming only 7 ounces of formula from a bottle.
He then got another bottle but barely ate from it.
He was offered cereal but refused it.
He appears irritable and has difficulty sleeping, fighting it hard to go to sleep.
His energy level is variable.
His older sister has been sick, and he was exposed to her.
He also had a date with his nephew who had a sinus infection.
Objective:
Vital signs: Temperature: 100.3°F (rectal)
Physical exam: The patient's ears looked okay during the examination.
The physical exam findings from the auto-text are not mentioned in the transcript, so this section is incomplete.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is a 1-year-old male with a viral upper respiratory infection.
Problem list: Viral upper respiratory infection; suspected sinus infection; cradle cap.
Plan:
I advised the patient's caregiver to continue with fluids, rest, and to watch for a barking cough.
I prescribed Zarbee's for the stuffy nose, and in case of a barking cough, I instructed the caregiver to give the patient a half a teaspoon of his sister's medicine.
I also recommended using Zarbee's for the regular cough, following the instructions for the dose for kids under a year of age.
I advised against giving honey to the patient due to the risk of botulism spores.

I recommended using a shampoo containing salicylic acid (such as Head and Shoulders, Selsun Blue, or Nizoral A.D.) to treat the cradle cap, using it twice a week, and avoiding the eyes when rinsing the hair.
Follow-up:
The patient will be seen again in 1 week.
